blocks.text: Baboon detects MCC editing kits from the loaded kit root or from a folder named after a game id.

items: Supported kits: Halo CE, Halo 2, Halo 3, Halo 3: ODST, Halo: Reach, Halo 4, and Halo 2: Anniversary multiplayer.
Recognized roots include HCEEK, H1EK, H2EK, H3EK, H3ODSTEK, HREK, H4EK, H2AMPEK, and H2AEK.
Use File > Settings to map a custom editing-kit folder name to a game when your layout is non-standard.
Per-game schemas are loaded from definitions/<game>/*.json next to Baboon.exe in release builds.

blocks.text: Baboon can open a single tag, a loose tags folder, or a monolithic cache. Loading runs on a background thread so the UI stays responsive.

items: Use File > Load Folder to choose the root of your editing kit, such as H3EK, HREK, H4EK, H3ODSTEK, or H2AMPEK/H2AEK.
Baboon will load the kit's tags folder from that root. Picking the kit root also lets the terminal, launcher buttons, and tool import commands resolve the correct working directory.
Choosing the tags folder directly works for browsing tags, but the kit root is the safest habit when you want Baboon to work with external tools.
Loose folder trees load lazily as you expand directories, while a background scan builds the full index for global tools.
Halo 4 monolithic blob_index.dat caches open read-only and can be browsed as tag entries.
Tag files are detected by probing the BLAM or MALB header, so non-tag files are skipped.

blocks.text: The browser can show the on-disk folder tree or regroup tags by tag group, with icons and friendly names from the definitions.

items: Recent folders appear in File for quick reopening.
Search supports space-separated AND terms, | OR terms, and ^prefix, suffix$, or ^exact$ anchors.
Sort each folder or group naturally, by name, or by type.
Reveal in tree jumps the browser to a tag and opens its parent folders.
Background indexing is persisted per game under Baboon's app data folder so later launches can skip the full scan.
Right-click tags in the browser to open actions for that tag.
Render models, models, scenarios, BSPs, collision models, and physics models can expose geometry extraction actions from this menu.
Model animation graph tags can expose animation extraction actions from this menu.
Bitmap tags can extract bitmap images, and monolithic cache entries can extract raw tag files.
Folder context menus can run batch actions such as JSON dump or bulk extraction where supported.
Field-value search finds text inside tag field values, optionally scoped to a tag group, and opens results in a clickable window.
Find References lists every indexed tag that references the current tag.
Content Explorer shows the current tag's parents and children as a reference graph with history and filtering.
Unreferenced tag scans help find tags that nothing else points at.
Keyword tagging stores freeform labels in a sidecar outside the tags and lets you browse or filter by them.
Scenario map ID and sound tools expose common kit-wide lists without hand-searching raw tags.
Tag Diff compares the current tag against another open tab or a tag on disk and can export differences as TSV.
Open tags appear as tabs with their group icon.
Tabs with unsaved edits show an amber tint and marker.
Tear a tab into a floating resizable window, then dock it back into the tab rack.
Baboon keeps an LRU cache of parsed tags so large sessions do not keep every document loaded forever.

blocks.text: Loose little-endian tags can be edited inline. Monolithic-cache tags and big-endian tags open read-only.

items: Edit scalars, integers, reals, strings, string_ids, enums, flags, and colors.
Tag references can be opened directly; Alt-click opens the target in a floating window.
Drag a tag from the browser onto a reference field to set it, and missing references are highlighted.
Tag-reference rows for render_model, collision_model, physics_model, and model_animation_graph can show an Import button.
Import runs the matching editing-kit tool command from the kit root, so it needs a loaded editing-kit folder.
For animation graphs, Baboon uses the model-animations-uncompressed tool command.
Block-index fields render as a dropdown of target elements plus a go-to control.
Field docs, units, ranges, and explanation blocks come from the JSON definitions.
Undo and redo cover field edits and structural block edits.
Search fields collapses the editor to matching fields and their ancestors.
Expert mode reveals advanced fields that are normally hidden.
Blocks support add, insert, duplicate, delete, and delete all where the tag format allows count changes.
Fixed-size arrays omit count-changing actions but still support copy and replace.
Right-click a block name in the editor header to copy and paste block data.
Copy element copies the selected block entry. Copy entire block copies every entry in that block.
Paste, Replace selected element, and Replace entire block appear when the clipboard is compatible with the current tag group and block path.
Copy block as TSV and Paste TSV let leaf fields round-trip through a spreadsheet.
Click or focus a block's element selector, then use the mouse wheel to move up and down through entries.
Arrow Up and Arrow Down also move through the selected block entries.
The < and > buttons beside the selector do the same one-entry step when you prefer clicking.
Nested blocks include jump-to-parent navigation with the path shown on hover.
Shader, material, and material_shader tags use a Guerilla-style parameter grid instead of only the raw field tree.
Baboon resolves render-method definitions and options, then caches them across tags.
Bitmap, scalar, integer, color, and category parameters show their defaults and edit inline.
Bitmap-reference rows can show thumbnails with a larger hover preview.
Changed rows get a differs-from-default indicator and can be reset from the context menu.
The label column is resizable, and full parameter names and types are shown on hover.
Optional animated parameters can be added from context menus and edited through their functions.
Tag mapping functions can be edited as identity, constant, linear, or linear key functions.
Curve points, color graphs, and input or range string_ids are exposed through focused controls.
Common inputs such as time, frame, random, and shield vitality are available for selection.
The raw hex blob path preserves arbitrary function data losslessly when Baboon cannot present every detail structurally.
Bitmap tags decode to an RGBA preview inside Baboon.
Use image and mip selectors to inspect multi-image bitmaps and mip levels.
Toggle R, G, B, and A channels, including alpha-only inspection.
Zoom to cursor, drag to pan, use preset zoom levels, or fit the image.
The preview reports pixel coordinates, RGBA values, format, dimensions, and image count.
Open a .model tag and choose the Render model tab to inspect the referenced render_model without scrolling through the field tree.
Model and render_model tags can render a real-time 3D preview with orbit, pan, and zoom camera controls.
Use the variant selector to inspect named variants and their region or permutation choices.
Marker overlays can be filtered, and render_model marker fields and names can be edited inline.
The Viewport slider in the render tab scales the preview from 80% to 260%; the value is global and saved in preferences.
If the preview becomes too wide for the editor panel, the region and variant controls move below it automatically.

blocks.text: Sound, dialogue, and sound_looping tags can be auditioned in the editor without leaving Baboon.

items: Halo CE inline Ogg, Halo 2 inline Opus, Xbox IMA ADPCM, PCM, Halo 3/ODST/Reach FMOD banks, and Halo 4/H2A Wwise packages are resolved by game.
Use per-permutation or per-event play controls, Stop, volume, language selection, and status readouts.
Decoded audio and external banks or packages are cached lazily.
Dialogue and sound_looping tags expose play and extraction controls for referenced sounds.
Extract one sound or all sounds to WAV under the data folder structure that tool expects; Halo CE can also extract original Ogg streams.
Curated overview panels summarize tags that are tedious as raw field dumps.
material_effects, dialogue, and sound_classes include cross-game tables where available.
Clickable references in overview tables jump to related tags.
Save colors from the color editor into reusable Baboon palettes.
Reload palettes in other tags to keep shader and material colors consistent.
Palette sidecars are stored outside the tag files.

blocks.text: Export work runs on background threads and reports progress through the status bar.

items: Dump a single tag or folder subtree to pretty-printed JSON.
Extract raw tag files, including entries from monolithic caches.
Extract bitmap images to TIFF individually or in bulk across a folder.
Extract model, render_model, collision_model, physics_model, scenario_structure_bsp, and scenario geometry to JMS or ASS where supported.
Import info and animation extraction run through the companion blam-tag-shell tools.
Import buttons on geometry and animation references run the matching tool verb against the source asset.
The integrated terminal runs commands in the editing-kit root with live streamed output.
Terminal open or closed state is remembered per editing kit.
Toolbar buttons launch the loaded kit's Sapien, tag_test, and Blender when their executables are available.
Blender uses the path configured in File > Settings.
Run Tool Command lists each game's tool commands from per-game JSON and builds a form for the selected command.
Command forms use dropdowns, path pickers, and validation for required parameters before running.
Assembled commands run in the integrated terminal.
Use File > Settings > Appearance to switch Dark mode on or off and adjust UI scale.
Appearance settings are saved in Baboon's preferences, so they stay set the next time you launch the app.
The same Appearance section also has the default Model viewport size used by .model render previews.
Settings also cover browser mode, browser prefixes, double-click behavior, expert mode, scroll wheel dropdown behavior, Blender path, and custom editing-kit aliases.
Recent folders, keyword sidecars, palette sidecars, last-session restore preference, and per-kit terminal state are persisted under Baboon's app data folder.
